messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Introduction to Statistics in R course - online, 19-22 January
messages.subject_clean: Introduction to Statistics in R course - online, 19-22 January
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
start the new year by building strong foundations in  statistics with R!
 
Our online course  "Introduction to Statistics in R" will take place on 19?22 January and is designed for complete beginners, combining clear explanations with hands-on practice in R. 
 
This course will help you read, interpret, and write R scripts, apply key statistical tools to research questions, and perform efficient, reproducible analyses. By the end, you'll confidently use R for both descriptive and inferential statistics.
 
Only a few seats are still available:  [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/fmc/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/fmc/ ) 
 
Happy New Year everyone!
 
Best regards,
 
Carlo
 
 

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] LiDAR Data Analysis in R - online, 26-28 January
messages.subject_clean: LiDAR Data Analysis in R - online, 26-28 January
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
registration is still open for the upcoming online course: LiDAR Data Analysis in R
 Dates: 26?28 January 2026 Format: Online Schedule: 1:00?5:00 pm (Berlin time)
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/lidar-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/lidar-r/ )
 
This 3-day course introduces the key principles of LiDAR technology and its ecological applications. Participants will learn to work with LiDAR data using the lidR package in R through a combination of lectures and hands-on practical sessions.
 
The course covers point cloud processing, terrain modeling, classification, and extraction of ecological metrics. It is designed for ecologists, researchers, and professionals with basic knowledge of R.
 
If you are interested, there is still time to register.
Best regards, Carlo
 
 
 
 

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Unlock Deep Learning Skills with Live Online Training in R and Python
messages.subject_clean: Unlock Deep Learning Skills with Live Online Training in R and Python
messages.body_plain:
Unlock Deep Learning Skills with Live Online Training in Python and R

Deep learning is at the core of modern data science, powering breakthroughs
in image analysis, natural language processing, pattern recognition and
predictive modelling. Whether you prefer Python or R, we now offer two
dedicated live online courses that teach deep learning from first
principles with hands-on implementations using real data.

---

Deep Learning Using Python (DLUP01)
https://prstats.org/course/deep-learning-using-python-dlup01/

A practical introduction to deep learning using Python and PyTorch. Learn
foundational concepts ? neural networks, backpropagation, optimization ?
and build modern models such as multilayer perceptrons (MLPs),
convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transformers.

Gain experience implementing models in PyTorch with real datasets and
explore state-of-the-art workflows for text and image tasks.

Dates: 14-15 April 2026
Format: Live online (recordings available)
Duration: 2 days, 6 hours per day
Fee: ?300

Suitable for participants new to deep learning who want to learn practical,
research-ready Python workflows.

---

Deep Learning Using R (DLUR01)
https://prstats.org/course/deep-learning-using-r-dlur01/

A companion course in R, using the torch ecosystem to build and deploy deep
learning models.Covers the conceptual and mathematical foundations of
neural networks, and walks through implementations of MLPs, CNNs and
transformer architectures directly in R. Includes hands-on coding,
practical model training, evaluation and modern architectures for use in
real research workflows.

Dates: 23?24 April 2026
Format: Live online (recordings available)
Duration: 2 days, 6 hours per day
Fee: ?300

Ideal for R users who want to apply deep learning without switching
programming languages.Both courses provide interactive instruction,
practical coding exercises and worked examples that build confidence in
applying deep learning to real problems.

---

Each course uses recordings and supportive teaching formats to ensure
accessibility across time zones and backgrounds.

Learn more and register:
Deep Learning Using Python:
https://prstats.org/course/deep-learning-using-python-dlup01/
Deep Learning Using R:
https://prstats.org/course/deep-learning-using-r-dlur01/

These courses are suitable for data scientists, researchers, analysts and
anyone seeking to develop practical deep learning expertise in their
preferred programming environment.


Please email oliver at prstats.org with any questions

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Peer Reviewing and Editing Journals – last chance
messages.subject_clean: Peer Reviewing and Editing Journals – last chance
messages.body_plain:
Hi everyone

Last chance to join our 1-day seminar Peer Reviewing and Editing Journals livestreaming January 8 with Carlton Fong (Department of Curriculum and Instruction, Texas State University). This seminar on peer reviewing and journal editing offers critical insights for ecologists and environmental scientists who utilize R in their research. Participants will learn to deliver rigorous, constructive reviews efficiently, directly enhancing their ability to evaluate and improve the quality of R-based data analysis and methodology in submitted manuscripts. Mastering these skills empowers researchers to actively shape the standards and direction of ecological and environmental scholarship, fostering best practices for the R community within their specific fields.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/from-peer-reviewing-to-editing-journals) to secure your spot, and feel free to share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Species Distribution Modelling With Bayesian Statistics (SDMB07) - Take your species-distribution modelling skills into the Bayesian realm!
messages.subject_clean: Species Distribution Modelling With Bayesian Statistics (SDMB07) - Take your species-distribution modelling skills into the Bayesian realm!
messages.body_plain:
*Take your species-distribution modelling skills into the Bayesian realm!*

*Species Distribution Modelling With Bayesian Statistics (SDMB07)*

https://prstats.org/course/species-distribution-modelling-with-bayesian-statistics-sdmb07/

Join our intensive *three-day live online workshop*: *Species Distribution
Modelling With Bayesian Statistics (SDMB07)*. If you have working knowledge
of species distribution modelling and R and want to deepen your ability to
handle uncertainty, interpret predictions and apply advanced Bayesian
workflows, this course is for you.

*What you will learn*

   -

   How to apply the Bayesian method Bayesian Additive Regression Trees
   (BART) in R for species distribution models, exploiting its strengths in
   prediction accuracy and uncertainty surfaces.
   -

   How to conduct full Bayesian workflows: data preparation, variable/
   predictor selection, model fitting, mapping credible intervals and local
   prediction uncertainty.
   -

   How to handle model interpretation and diagnostics in a Bayesian
   context: posterior summaries, credible intervals, uncertainty surfaces and
   model robustness.
   -

   Hands-on R coding sessions including annotated scripts you can adapt for
   your own species of interest.

*Who should attend*

   -

   Researchers, ecologists, conservation scientists, and spatial-modelling
   practitioners with previous experience in species distribution modelling
   and R.
   -

   Anyone who wants to move beyond standard SDM frameworks into Bayesian
   methods that provide richer inference, improved uncertainty estimation, and
   more robust predictive performance.
   -

   Practitioners working in ecology, biogeography, conservation planning or
   any applied field that requires spatial predictions with quantified
   uncertainty.

*Format & logistics*

   -

   Live online format ? you can participate from anywhere with internet
   access.
   -

   Three full days of training, each day with four hours of interactive
   instruction and practical exercises.
   -

   Sessions will be recorded and available afterward ? making it easier to
   follow up or catch up across time-zones.

*Why this course stands out*

   -

   It introduces the state-of-the-art Bayesian modelling technique BART,
   which recent research shows provides strong performance in species
   distribution modelling.
   -

   Focus on uncertainty quantification: rather than a point-prediction only
   approach, the course equips you to map credible intervals and interpret
   confidence in predictions.
   -

   Hands-on, applicable: you leave the course with code and workflows that
   you can adapt directly to your species modelling projects.

*How to register / next steps*
Visit the PR Stats website for full details, upcoming dates and
registration. Early registration is recommended as places are limited. If
you have any questions about suitability, schedule, or prerequired
experience, email oliver at prstats.org with any questions
https://prstats.org/course/species-distribution-modelling-with-bayesian-statistics-sdmb07/

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Introductory & Advanced Courses in Geometric Morphometrics
messages.subject_clean: Introductory & Advanced Courses in Geometric Morphometrics
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
We are excited to offer an online Introductory Geometric Morphometrics course on 3?5 February . This course covers the essentials of data acquisition, quality control, and basic analysis in 2D and 3D geometric morphometrics, using easy-to-use software and practical hands-on sessions. It?s ideal for beginners and researchers seeking a solid foundation.
 
Course website:[ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/course22/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/course22/ ) 
 
 
 
For those interested in more advanced analyses, we also offer the companion course:
Multidimensional Phenotypic Evolution 2?6 March (online)
This advanced course explores techniques for analyzing multidimensional traits?including geometric morphometric and non-morphometric data?at micro- and macroevolutionary scales. It includes more complex statistical approaches and practical sessions using R and graphical tools.
Both courses are taught by Dr. Carmelo Fruciano (University of Catania, Italy) and form a complementary learning pathway from data acquisition to in-depth phenotypic analysis.
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/mpe/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/mpe/ ) 
 
Feel free to contact us for more details.
Best regards, Carlo

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] new R ecology book
messages.subject_clean: new R ecology book
messages.body_plain:
Hi folks,

The latest Springer UseR! volume, R Coding for Ecology, was released this week.

Book:
https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-99665-8

Code:
https://github.com/RCodingForEcology

Edited by Duccio Rocchini, this book is devoted to ecological data
analysis with R, with examples created by the developers of the
described packages. R software is now an important reality in
ecological informatics. Specific packages have been developed to
manage, analyse and visualize ecological data. This book is a
comprehensive catalogue of ecological R packages with running
examples! This step-by-step procedure book is aimed at students and
researchers with previous experience in R with a passion for coding
their own ecological analyses.

Sarah
-- 
Sarah Goslee (she/her)
http://www.numberwright.com

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Course  - Handling Missing Data in R - online, 22nd-24th April)
messages.subject_clean: Course  - Handling Missing Data in R - online, 22nd-24th April)
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
Missing data can seriously affect your analyses?but knowing how to handle it properly makes all the difference.
Join our Introduction to Missing Data course, a practical 3-day online workshop (22?24 April, 15:00?18:00), where you?ll learn to:

Diagnose missingness mechanisms (MCAR, MAR, MNAR)

Visualize and explore missing data patterns in R

Apply both traditional and advanced imputation methods like Multiple Imputation, Soft Impute, and Expectation?Maximization
 
For more information, please visit: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/missing-data-with-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/missing-data-with-r/ ) 
 
Best regards,
Carlo
 
 

--------------------

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] RNA-Seq Analysis - Unlock the full potential of your transcriptome data
messages.subject_clean: RNA-Seq Analysis - Unlock the full potential of your transcriptome data
messages.body_plain:
RNA-Seq Analysis

RNA-Seq analysis training ? live online course covering experiment design,
data QC, alignment, gene expression, DESeq2 differential expression, PCA,
visualisation, and functional analysis.


*ONLY 5 PLACES LEFT!*

https://prstats.org/course/rna-seq-analysis-rnaa01/


Join our four-day live online workshop: *RNA-Seq Analysis (RNAA01)*. If
you?re working with RNA-Seq or planning to generate bulk transcriptome
data, and you want to take your analysis from raw reads through to
biological insight, this is the course for you.

*What you will learn*

   -

   How to design a robust RNA-Seq experiment and avoid common pitfalls.
   -

   How to perform quality control on raw RNA-Seq data, align reads, and
   assess alignment quality.
   -

   How to quantify gene expression and apply the widely-used DESeq2
   workflow for differential expression analysis.
   -

   How to visualise high-throughput data using principal component analysis
   (PCA), volcano plots, MA plots and other techniques.
   -

   How to perform functional interpretation of differential expression
   results, turning numbers into biological insight.

*Who should attend*

   -

   Researchers, postgraduate students and industry professionals working
   with bulk RNA-Seq data.
   -

   Anyone who has basic experience with R, RStudio and the fundamentals of
   molecular biology or transcriptomics.
   -

   Those who want to move from raw sequence data to actionable biological
   conclusions with confidence.

*Course format*

   -

   Four days of live online sessions (approximately 3? hours each day) in a
   UK / Western European time zone.
   -

   Interactive lectures, hands-on practical exercises and dedicated
   discussion time.
   -

   Course materials, code and datasets will be provided, and participants
   are encouraged to bring their own data for discussion when possible.
   -

   Recordings of sessions available after each day for participants across
   different time zones.

*Why this course stands out*

   -

   It offers an end-to-end workflow from design through QC, alignment,
   differential expression and functional analysis ? designed specifically for
   researchers working in transcriptomics.
   -

   It emphasises not just how to run the tools, but how to interpret
   results, understand the assumptions and recognise where things can go wrong.
   -

   Designed by experienced bioinformaticians, the course balances theory,
   practice and discussion in an accessible live-online format.

*How to register / next steps*
Visit the PR Stats website for full course details, upcoming dates and
registration information. Early registration is advisable, as places may be
limited. For any questions about suitability, prerequisites or logistics,
please contact the course organisers.
https://prstats.org/course/rna-seq-analysis-rnaa01/

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Statistical Analysis of Social Network Data – last chance
messages.subject_clean: Statistical Analysis of Social Network Data – last chance
messages.body_plain:
Hi everyone

Last chance to join our 1-day seminar Statistical Analysis of Social Network Data livestreaming January 15 with Tianxi Li (School of Statistics, University of Minnesota). This seminar on statistical analysis of social network data offers powerful tools for ecologists and environmental scientists working with complex relational structures. Participants will learn to model species interactions, habitat connectivity, or resource flow using modern R packages, enabling deeper insights into ecological processes and dependencies. The hands-on R demonstrations will equip researchers with reproducible workflows to analyze network data, test hypotheses, and generate publishable findings in their own ecological studies.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/statistical-analysis-of-social-network-d) to secure your spot, and feel free to share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling in R course
messages.subject_clean: Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling in R course
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
There are only a few seats remain for the online course Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling in R,  taking place 19?23 January.
This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the theory and practice of SDM and ENM, combining conceptual foundations with hands-on modelling in R. Participants will learn how to build, validate, and apply models using their own species occurrence data, while understanding key limitations, assumptions, and best practices.
 
Students, researchers, and practitioners interested in applying SDM/ENM in ecology, biogeography, and conservation. Basic familiarity with R is recommended; all scripts are provided and fully explained.
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/course45/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/course45/ )

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Machine Learning for Computational Biology 2.0 – last chance
messages.subject_clean: Machine Learning for Computational Biology 2.0 – last chance
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance to join our 1-day seminar Machine Learning for Computational Biology 2.0 livestreaming January 13 with Nikolay Oskolkov (Group Leader (PI) at LIOS). This workshop offers a comprehensive exploration of machine learning techniques, providing advanced analytical tools for complex data interpretation. Participants will gain practical skills in implementing algorithms such as k-means clustering, Gaussian Mixture Models, and Markov Chain Monte Carlo methods in R and Python, directly applicable to analyzing ecological and environmental datasets. These competencies will enable researchers to develop and optimize sophisticated models for their own studies, enhancing their ability to uncover patterns and make robust inferences from diverse data sources.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/machine-learning-for-computational-biolo) to secure your spot, and feel free to share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Advance Your Ecological Data Analysis with Causal Inference
messages.subject_clean: Advance Your Ecological Data Analysis with Causal Inference
messages.body_plain:
ausal Inference for Ecologists (CIFE01)

Causal Inference for Ecologists is an applied R course teaching researchers
how to identify and estimate causal effects in ecological and environmental
data.

https://prstats.org/course/causal-inference-for-ecologists-cife01/


*23?27 March 2026 - **Live Online*

Do you work with ecological data and need to move beyond correlation to
understand cause-and-effect? Join our five-day *Causal Inference for
Ecologists* course and gain the practical tools to answer real causal
questions using both experimental and observational datasets.

In this course, you will learn how to:

   -

   Construct and interpret *Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)* to formalise
   causal assumptions.
   -

   Identify and avoid bias from confounders and colliders.
   -

   Understand why traditional model selection methods like AIC can mislead
   causal analysis.
   -

   Apply causal inference frameworks to your own ecological research
   problems.

Our live online format includes lectures, hands-on practical exercises, and
open discussions. All sessions are recorded and made available to
participants across time zones.

*Who Should Attend:* Quantitative scientists with experience in R who are
testing hypotheses, estimating causal effects, or building predictive
models.

*Software:* We will work with *lme4* and *rstanarm*, covering both
frequentist and Bayesian modelling approaches.

*Secure your place today and transform the way you answer causal questions
in ecological research.*
Register at *prstats.org/course/causal-inference-for-ecologists-cife01/
<http://prstats.org/course/causal-inference-for-ecologists-cife01/>*
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions
-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]
messages.body_snippet:
ausal Inference for Ecologists (CIFE01)

Causal Inference for Ecologists is an applied R course teaching researchers
how to identify and estimate causal effects in ecological and environmental
data.

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Interactive Data Applications with Shiny (SHID01)
messages.subject_clean: Interactive Data Applications with Shiny (SHID01)
messages.body_plain:
Interactive Data Applications with Shiny (SHID01)

Interactive Data Applications with Shiny is a practical R Shiny course for
researchers focused on building, customising, and deploying interactive web
applications from data analyses.

https://prstats.org/course/interactive-data-applications-with-shiny-shid01/


*28?29 April 2026 - **Live Online*

Bring your data to life with interactive, web-ready tools using *R Shiny*.
This two-day practical workshop teaches researchers how to build,
customise, and deploy interactive web applications that support data
exploration and statistical analysis?without requiring any prior web
development experience.

In this course you will learn to:

   -

   Understand Shiny?s structure and *reactive programming model* to connect
   user inputs to dynamic outputs.
   -

   Build user interfaces with a variety of input widgets (sliders,
   dropdowns, text inputs) and output types (plots, tables, formatted text).

   -

   Integrate interactive graphics and visualisations into Shiny apps using
   packages like *ggplot2*.
   -

   Debug reactive applications and structure code for clarity and
   maintainability.
   -

   Deploy your finished Shiny applications to the web using *shinyapps.io
   <http://shinyapps.io>*.

The live online format combines instructor-led code demonstrations with
hands-on exercises and opportunities to discuss your own research use
cases. All sessions are recorded and made available to participants across
time zones.

*Who Should Attend:* Researchers and data scientists comfortable using R
who want to create interactive dashboards or applications that facilitate
data exploration, communication, and stakeholder engagement.

*Course Benefits:*

   -

   Build the skills to prototype and deploy Shiny applications from your R
   analyses
   -

   Improve accessibility of your results for collaborators and stakeholders
   -

   Gain confidence in reactive programming and interactive interface design

*Secure your place now:*
Register at *prstats.org/course/interactive-data-applications-with-shiny-shid01/
<http://prstats.org/course/interactive-data-applications-with-shiny-shid01/>*

For enquiries, contact *oliver at prstats.org*

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Online live course: Functional trait diversity: calculating and interpreting a key component of biodiversity
messages.subject_clean: Online live course: Functional trait diversity: calculating and interpreting a key component of biodiversity
messages.body_plain:
Dear colleagues,

Transmitting Science is offering a new edition of the course "Functional 
trait diversity: calculating and interpreting a key component of 
biodiversity".

Dates & schedule: live online sessions on February 3rd, 5th, 10th and 
12th, 2026, from 13:30 to 18:00 (Madrid time zone).

Course webpage: 
https://www.transmittingscience.com/courses/ecology/functional-trait-diversity-calculating-and-interpreting-a-key-component-of-biodiversity/ 
[1]

This course aims to introduce participants to metrics of functional 
trait diversity and related community-trait structure analyses, a key 
component of biodiversity that is increasingly important for 
understanding how biological communities respond to environmental change 
and the consequences for ecosystem functionality.

Instruction will combine lectures, hands-on computational exercises in 
R, and guided examples. Language of instruction: English.

For any questions, please write to courses at transmittingscience.com

Best regards,

Sole

-- 
Soledad De Esteban-Trivigno, PhD
Director
Transmitting Science
www.transmittingscience.com/courses

Bluesky @soledeesteban.bsky.social
X @SoleDeEsteban
Orcid: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-2049-0890

info at transmittingscience.com or 
http://transmittingscience.com/additional-terms. If you consider that 
the processing does not comply with current legislation, you can 
complain with the supervisory authority at www. aepd.es .
Confidentiality. - The content of this communication, as well as that of

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Single-Cell RNA-Seq Analysis
messages.subject_clean: Single-Cell RNA-Seq Analysis
messages.body_plain:
Single-Cell RNA-Seq Analysis

Live online training covering all stages of single-cell transcriptomic data
analysis ? from experimental design to data QC, normalization, clustering,
differential expression, and biological interpretation.

https://prstats.org/course/single-cell-rna-seq-analysis-scrn02/

Join our four-day live online workshop: Single-Cell RNA-Seq Analysis
(SCRN02). If you?re working with single-cell transcriptome data or planning
to dive into single-cell workflows, this course will guide you from raw
data to interpretable biological insight.

What you will learn

How to design a robust single-cell RNA-Seq experiment, including strategies
for cell capture, sequencing depth and batch control. How to perform
quality control and filtering of single-cell data to ensure high-quality
downstream analysis. How to normalize and process data, cluster cells, and
identify cell types or states. How to conduct differential expression
analysis, trajectory inference or other advanced single-cell analyses. How
to interpret results biologically ? linking clusters, cell types or
trajectories to meaningful insights.

Who should attend

Researchers, postgraduate students and industry professionals working with
single-cell RNA-Seq data. Anyone with basic experience in R, data analysis,
and transcriptomics who wants to build confidence in single-cell workflows.
Those who want to confidently move from raw single-cell data to actionable
biological conclusions.

Course format

Four days of live online sessions (approximately 3? hours each day) in a UK
/ Western European time zone. Interactive lectures, hands-on practical
sessions and discussion time. Course materials, code and example datasets
will be provided ? participants are encouraged to bring their own data for
discussion when possible. Recordings available after each day to support
participants in different time zones.

Why this course stands out

It offers an end-to-end workflow specifically tailored for single-cell data
? from design through QC, clustering, differential expression and
biological interpretation. It emphasises not just how to run analysis
pipelines, but how to understand assumptions, interpret results, and avoid
common pitfalls in single-cell data analysis. Developed and delivered by
experienced bioinformaticians, the course balances theoretical foundations
with applied, hands-on training in a live-online format.

How to register / next steps

Visit the PR Stats website for full course details, upcoming dates and
registration information. Early registration is recommended, as places are
limited. https://prstats.org/course/single-cell-rna-seq-analysis-scrn02/

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Introduction to Generalised Linear Mixed Models for Ecologists
messages.subject_clean: Introduction to Generalised Linear Mixed Models for Ecologists
messages.body_plain:
Introduction to Generalised Linear Mixed Models for Ecologists
<https://prstats.org/course/introduction-to-generalised-linear-mixed-models-for-ecologists-mmie01/>Learn
to build and interpret linear, generalised linear, and multilevel models
for ecological data using R, lme4, and rstanarm in this five day applied
training course.

https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-multilevel-modelling-using-brms-for-ecologists-mmie02/

Join our intensive live-online workshop: *Bayesian Multilevel Modelling
using brms for Ecologists*. If you work with ecological data and want to
advance beyond standard regression into hierarchical modelling,
spatial/temporal structure and full Bayesian workflows, this course is for
you.

*What you will learn*

   -

   A full foundation in Bayesian inference: priors, posteriors, credible
   intervals and uncertainty quantification.
   -

   How to fit and interpret generalised linear models (GLMs) and
   multilevel/hierarchical models using the brms package in R.
   -

   How to specify meaningful priors for ecological parameters and perform
   model criticism via posterior predictive checks, WAIC, LOO-CV and other
   diagnostics.
   -

   How to model non-Gaussian ecological responses (counts, proportions,
   zero-inflation), and account for spatial and temporal autocorrelation in
   your hierarchical models.
   -

   How to build multivariate or joint species-distribution models,
   interpret group-level effects, visualise hierarchical structures and
   communicate results effectively.

*Who should attend*
This course is designed for ecologists, data analysts, postgraduate
researchers and early-career scientists who already have some experience
with R and basic statistical concepts (e.g., mean, variance, correlation,
linear regression). While prior exposure to GLMs is helpful, the essentials
will be reviewed and built upon.

*Format & logistics*

   -

   Live online sessions (5 days, approx. 6 hours per day) covering 30 hours
   of instruction, practical exercises and guided coding.
   -

   Sessions are recorded and made available so you can revisit content if
   you miss a live slot.
   -

   Participants are encouraged to bring their own ecological datasets where
   possible, for practical application.

*Why this course stands out*

   -

   It bridges the gap between theoretical Bayesian modelling and applied
   ecological data analysis, focusing on workflows that ecologists can use in
   real research settings.
   -

   Emphasis on interpretation, visualisation and clear communication of
   uncertainty ? not just model fitting.
   -

   Uses the brms package which allows flexible specification of
   hierarchical models while leveraging powerful backend tools (Stan) for
   Bayesian computation.

*How to register / next steps*
Visit the PR Stats website for full course details, upcoming dates and
registration information. Early registration is recommended as places may
be limited. If you have questions about suitability, software requirements
(R, RStudio, brms package) or data prerequisites, contact the course
organisers for guidance.
https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-multilevel-modelling-using-brms-for-ecologists-mmie02/

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Animal Movement with R courses
messages.subject_clean: Animal Movement with R courses
messages.body_plain:
Dear r-sig Ecology members,
I would like to share information about an upcoming online training course that may be of interest to members of this community: Introduction to Analysing Animal Movement with R
 
Dates: 16?18 February (online)

Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/mve-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/mve-r/ ) 
 
 
This 3-day course provides an introduction to analysing animal movement data using R. Participants will learn how to import, clean, and process movement datasets, identify outliers, compute fundamental movement metrics such as step lengths and directions, and conduct home-range analyses. The course concludes with an introduction to describing movement patterns using statistical distributions.
 
The course combines lectures and hands-on coding sessions designed for participants with basic knowledge of R and RStudio, and some familiarity with GIS concepts. Example datasets and code will be provided, and participants are encouraged to bring their own movement data if available.
 
Topics include:

Importing and managing animal movement data

Detecting and handling erroneous GPS fixes

Calculating movement metrics: steps, turns, net squared displacement

Home-range analysis and common estimators

Working with data from multiple animals

Describing movement patterns using statistical distributions
 
For a deeper dive into animal movement ecology, we recommend the companion course:
Habitat Selection of Animals from Telemetry Data with R
 
Dates: 20-23 April (online)
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/telemetry/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/telemetry/ )
A follow-up course focusing on advanced techniques and detailed statistical methods for analysing animal habitat selection using telemetry data.
 
 
Best regards,Carlo
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D

info at physalia-courses.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Analyse Animal Movement Data Effectively & Confidently — From GPS to Ecology
messages.subject_clean: Analyse Animal Movement Data Effectively & Confidently — From GPS to Ecology
messages.body_plain:
Analyse Animal Movement Data Effectively & Confidently ? From GPS to Ecology

The Movement Ecology course provides a comprehensive, practical
introduction to analysing movement and tracking data across species ? from
ringing/CMR and VHF telemetry to GPS and path-tracking datasets. Whether
you're studying birds, mammals, reptiles or any mobile organisms, this
course gives you the conceptual foundation, statistical know-how, and
coding tools needed to turn raw tracking data into ecological inference.

https://prstats.org/course/movement-ecology-the-analysis-of-movement-data-move09/
What You Will Learn

   -

   The core concepts of movement ecology: how movement relates to
   behaviour, ecology, and the environment.
   -

   How to analyse movement trajectories ? from steps and turns to
   segmentation and classification of movement modes (e.g. home-range,
   dispersal, migration, nomadism).
   -

   Methods for estimating home ranges and utilisation distributions,
   creating isopleths, quantifying overlap, and defining core areas.
   -

   Tools to assess interactions and dynamic movement metrics, such as
   first-passage time or residence time, and to study how individuals move
   within space and time.
   -

   Techniques for resource and habitat-selection analyses: integrating
   spatial data with movement metrics, conducting step-selection or
   resource-selection models to infer habitat use and movement decisions.
   -

   How to choose appropriate tracking technology, considering motion
   capacity, environmental context, and research questions ? enabling you to
   design robust movement studies rather than just analysing data.

Format & Structure

   -

   The course runs for *5 days, 8 hours per day* (live online).
   -

   It combines lectures, theory, and hands-on practical exercises in R ?
   giving you direct experience working with real movement datasets.
   -

   Designed for ecologists, conservation biologists, wildlife researchers,
   and anyone working with telemetry, tagging, tracking or spatial movement
   data.

Why This Course Matters

Tracking and movement data have become central to understanding animal
ecology: migration, habitat use, dispersal, social interactions,
conservation, and responses to environmental change. The analytical demand
is high ? and simple descriptive statistics often fall short. This course
bridges the gap by offering a unified framework that combines ecology,
behaviour, movement theory and modern statistical methods.

Movements are rarely random: internal state, motion and navigation
capacity, and external environment all shape how and where animals move.
Understanding these drivers ? not just plotting tracks ? is essential for
conservation, management, population dynamics or behavioural ecology.

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]
messages.body_snippet:
Analyse Animal Movement Data Effectively & Confidently ? From GPS to Ecology

The Movement Ecology course provides a comprehensive, practical
introduction to analysing movement and tracking data acros

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Advanced Bayesian Modelling with R-INLA — 5 Days Intensive
messages.subject_clean: Advanced Bayesian Modelling with R-INLA — 5 Days Intensive
messages.body_plain:
Advanced Bayesian Modelling with R-INLA ? 5 Days Intensive
https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-modelling-using-r-inla-bmin03/

*Bayesian Modelling using R-INLA (BMIN03)* is a comprehensive, live-online
course that equips you with the theory and practical skills to build, fit,
and interpret Bayesian models using the power of the R-INLA package.
Perfect for applied researchers, analysts, and data scientists working in
R, this five-day course covers everything from foundational Bayesian
inference to advanced latent-effect modelling.

*Next course date:* 23?27 February 2026 (live online)
What You Will Learn

   -

   Core principles of *Bayesian inference* and how the INLA methodology
   compares with classical MCMC-based approaches.
   -

   How to *fit generalized and mixed models in R-INLA*, including linear,
   generalized, multilevel, time-series, and spatial models.
   -

   How to *define custom priors and latent effects*, even those not
   provided out-of-the-box ? giving you flexibility for complex or novel
   modelling tasks.
   -

   How to *interpret INLA output*, extract posterior summaries, and apply
   Bayesian modelling with confidence.
   -

   Hands-on experience working with real data and the option to explore
   your own datasets, under guidance.

Course Format & Structure

   -

   *Duration:* 5 days ? 7 hours per day.
   -

   *Format:* Live online sessions, recorded for later access so you can
   learn flexibly.
   -

   *Interactive:* Each day blends lectures with practical coding exercises
   and Q&A ? including sessions for participants to work on their own data.
   -

   *Support:* Complete course materials (slides, code, datasets), and
   30-day post-course support for follow-up questions or problems.

Who Should Attend

   -

   Researchers, analysts, or data scientists already using R who want to
   expand their toolkit with Bayesian methods and latent-effect modelling.
   -

   Those familiar with statistics and data analysis (e.g., linear or mixed
   models) but new to Bayesian inference or INLA.
   -

   Anyone needing flexible, efficient Bayesian modelling for complex data
   structures ? including hierarchical, time-series, and spatial data.

*Next course date:* 23?27 February 2026 (live online)
*Course fee:* ?500

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]
messages.body_snippet:
Advanced Bayesian Modelling with R-INLA ? 5 Days Intensive
https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-modelling-using-r-inla-bmin03/

*Bayesian Modelling using R-INLA (BMIN03)* is a comprehensive, live-onlin

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Bayesian Statistical Modelling with Stan and brms (BMSB01)
messages.subject_clean: Bayesian Statistical Modelling with Stan and brms (BMSB01)
messages.body_plain:
Bayesian Statistical Modelling with Stan and brms (BMSB01)
<https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-statistical-modelling-with-stan-and-brms-bmsb01/>

Bayesian Statistical Modelling with Stan and brms is an advanced R course
for researchers covering Bayesian model building, diagnostics, and
interpretation using Stan and brms.

*Advance Your Statistical Expertise with Bayesian Modelling in R*
*Bayesian Statistical Modelling with Stan and brms (BMSB01)*

https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-statistical-modelling-with-stan-and-brms-bmsb01/

*Next Offering: *Live online *5?7 May 2026* (UK/GMT+1)

Take your data analysis skills to the next level with this comprehensive
three-day live workshop designed for empirical researchers who want to
confidently apply Bayesian statistical methods using modern computational
tools in R. Delivered online with recorded sessions, this course offers both
 *conceptual foundations* and *hands-on practice* with real datasets and
workflows.

*What You Will Learn:*
? Core principles of Bayesian inference and how they differ from classical
approaches.
? How to apply Bayesian reasoning and Bayes? rule to real data problems.
? Practical model building using *Stan* and the *brms* R package,
leveraging Bayesian regression, generalized models, and hierarchical
structures.
? Model diagnostics, prior specification, posterior inference, and model
comparison with tools like WAIC and LOO.
? Extensions to robust regression, heteroskedastic modeling, logistic and
count data models, and Bayesian mixed effects models.

*Format & Features:*
? *Duration:* 3 days (6 hours per day), live online with recordings
available.
? *Interactive learning:* Combines lecture, coding exercises, and guided
discussions.
? *Materials included:* Code, datasets, and slides provided to all
participants.
? *Support:* Continued email support for 30 days post-course.
? *Prerequisites:* Familiarity with fitting linear or generalized linear
models in R recommended.

*Who Should Attend:*
This course is ideal for researchers in the social, behavioural,
biological, and applied sciences who want to deepen their understanding of
Bayesian inference and learn to use *Stan*?a state-of-the-art probabilistic
programming language?with the user-friendly *brms* interface.

Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions.


-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Live online course Introduction to Bayesian Analysis with R
messages.subject_clean: Live online course Introduction to Bayesian Analysis with R
messages.body_plain:
Dear colleagues,

Transmitting Science is offering the course "Introduction to Bayesian 
Inference in Practice".

Course webpage: 
https://www.transmittingscience.com/courses/statistics-and-bioinformatics/introduction-bayesian-inference-practice/

Instructors: Dr. Daniele Silvestro [1] (ETH Z?rich, Switzerland) and Dr. 
Tobias Andermann [2] (University of Uppsala, Sweden)

Course overview:

On this course, we will outline the relevant concepts and basic theory 
of Bayesian methods, but the focus of the course will be to learn how to 
perform Bayesian inference in practice. We will demonstrate how to 
implement the most common algorithms to estimate parameters based on 
posterior probabilities, such as Markov Chain Monte Carlo samplers, and 
how to build hierarchical models. We will also touch upon hypothesis 
testing using Bayes factors and Bayesian variable selection.

The course will take a learn-by-doing approach, in which participants 
will implement their own MCMCs using R or Python (templates for both 
languages will be provided).

After completion of the course, the participants will have gained a 
better understanding of how the main Bayesian methods implemented in 
many programs used in biological research work. Participants will also 
learn how to model at least basic problems using Bayesian statistics and 
how to implement the necessary algorithms to solve them. The aim is 
that, by the end of the course, each participant will have written their 
own MCMC - from scratch!

For more information, please contact us at 
courses at transmittingscience.com

Best wishes

Sole

-- 
Soledad De Esteban-Trivigno, PhD
Director
Transmitting Science
www.transmittingscience.com/courses

Bluesky @soledeesteban.bsky.social
X @SoleDeEsteban
Orcid: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-2049-0890



Links:
------
[1] https://www.transmittingscience.com/instructors/daniele-silvestro/
[2] https://www.transmittingscience.com/instructors/tobias-andermann/

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Machine Learning for Ecological Time Series using R (METR01)
messages.subject_clean: Machine Learning for Ecological Time Series using R (METR01)
messages.body_plain:
Machine Learning for Ecological Time Series (METR01)
<https://prstats.org/course/machine-learning-for-ecological-time-series-metr01/>
https://prstats.org/course/machine-learning-for-ecological-time-series-metr01/

Machine Learning for Ecological Time Series is an applied R course teaching
ecologists how to analyse, model, and predict ecological time series data.

Time series data are central to ecological and environmental research, from
monitoring population dynamics and climate signals to tracking ecosystem
responses to change. This intensive course provides a practical and
conceptually grounded introduction to state-of-the-art machine learning
approaches for analysing ecological time series, with a strong emphasis on
real-world application and best practice.

Delivered by experienced instructors, the course blends theory, hands-on
coding, and applied examples to help participants confidently apply machine
learning techniques to their own data.
------------------------------
Who Is This Course For?

This course is ideal for:

   -

   PhD and MSc students
   -

   Postdoctoral researchers
   -

   Principal investigators
   -

   Environmental and ecological professionals

Participants should have some prior experience with *Python*. Core concepts
will be reviewed at the start of the course, but a basic familiarity with
the language will help you get the most out of the practical sessions.
------------------------------
What You Will Learn

By the end of the course, you will be able to:

   -

   Understand the strengths and limitations of machine learning for time
   series analysis
   -

   Prepare, explore, and preprocess ecological time series data
   -

   Apply supervised and unsupervised machine learning methods to temporal
   data
   -

   Build, validate, and interpret predictive models
   -

   Avoid common pitfalls such as overfitting, data leakage, and improper
   validation
   -

   Critically assess when machine learning is (and is not) appropriate for
   ecological inference

------------------------------
Course Highlights

   -

   *Ecology-focused examples*: Methods are taught using realistic
   ecological and environmental datasets
   -

   *Hands-on learning*: Practical coding exercises throughout, with
   reusable workflows
   -

   *Best-practice modelling*: Emphasis on reproducibility, validation
   strategies, and interpretability
   -

   *Expert support*: Live Q&A sessions with instructors to discuss concepts
   and troubleshoot code
   -

   *Flexible delivery*: Recorded lectures combined with live sessions
   -

   *Certificate of completion*: Digital certificate provided at the end of
   the course

------------------------------
Course Format

   -

   *5-day online course*
   -

   Pre-recorded lectures released daily
   -

   Live Q&A sessions for discussion and clarification
   -

   Continued access to course materials and recordings after completion

------------------------------
Why Take This Course?

Machine learning is increasingly used in ecological research, but applying
these methods responsibly requires more than simply fitting models. This
course equips you with both the conceptual understanding and practical
skills needed to use machine learning effectively, transparently, and
confidently in ecological time series analysis.
*Harness modern machine learning methods to analyse, model, and interpret
ecological time series data - Book today!*

*Email oliver at prstats.org <oliver at prstats.org> with any questions*

*You may also be interested in *
Machine Learning for Time Series (MLTP01)
<https://prstats.org/course/machine-learning-for-time-series-mltp01/>
https://prstats.org/course/machine-learning-for-time-series-mltp01/

Machine Learning for Time Series is a practical Python course teaching how
to model, analyse, and forecast time series data using machine learning
methods.


-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

using brms for Ecologists*. If you work with ecological data and want to
advance beyond standard regression into hierarchical modelling,

   -

   How to specify meaningful priors for ecological parameters and perform
   model criticism via posterior predictive checks, WAIC, LOO-CV and other
   diagnostics.
   -

   How to model non-Gaussian ecological responses (counts, proportions,
   zero-inflation), and account for spatial and temporal autocorrelation in
   your hierarchical models.
   -

   How to build multivariate or joint species-distribution models,
   interpret group-level effects, visualise hierarchical structures and
   communicate results effectively.

*Who should attend*
This course is designed for ecologists, data analysts, postgraduate
researchers and early-career scientists who already have some experience
with R and basic statistical concepts (e.g., mean, variance, correlation,
linear regression). While prior exposure to GLMs is helpful, the essentials
will be reviewed and built upon.

*Format & logistics*

   -

   Live online sessions (5 days, approx. 6 hours per day) covering 30 hours
   of instruction, practical exercises and guided coding.
   -

   Sessions are recorded and made available so you can revisit content if
   you miss a live slot.
   -

   Participants are encouraged to bring their own ecological datasets where
   possible, for practical application.

*Why this course stands out*

   -

   It bridges the gap between theoretical Bayesian modelling and applied
   ecological data analysis, focusing on workflows that ecologists can use in
   real research settings.
   -

   Emphasis on interpretation, visualisation and clear communication of
   uncertainty ? not just model fitting.
   -

   Uses the brms package which allows flexible specification of
   hierarchical models while leveraging powerful backend tools (Stan) for
   Bayesian computation.

*How to register / next steps*
Visit the PR Stats website for full course details, upcoming dates and
registration information. Early registration is recommended as places may
be limited. If you have questions about suitability, software requirements
(R, RStudio, brms package) or data prerequisites, contact the course
organisers for guidance.
https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-multilevel-modelling-using-brms-for-ecologists-mmie02/

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Foundations of Statistical Models and Linear Regression – last chance
messages.subject_clean: Foundations of Statistical Models and Linear Regression – last chance
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance to join our 3-day seminar Foundations of Statistical Models and Linear Regression livestreaming February 3?5 with Centre for Applied Statistics Courses (CASC) (University College London). This seminar on Foundations of Statistical Models and Linear Regression provides essential skills for analyzing complex data, with hands-on practice using software including R. Ecologists can leverage these robust regression techniques to model intricate relationships within vegetation, soil, or hydrological datasets. Applying these advanced analytical methods will significantly strengthen the interpretability and predictive power of their environmental research.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/foundations-of-statistical-models-and-li) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] [COURSE] Marine Genomics  (25–29 May, Crete, Greece)
messages.subject_clean: [COURSE] Marine Genomics  (25–29 May, Crete, Greece)
messages.body_plain:
Dear r-sig-ecology members,
We are pleased to announce an upcoming in-person course on Marine Genomics, taking place 25?29 May at the Hellenic Centre for Marine Research (HCMR) in Heraklion, Crete (Greece).
 
This 5-day course provides a hands-on introduction to marine and population genomics, with a strong focus on practical data analysis.

 
Most analyses will be performed using UNIX command-line tools and R.
 
Participants will work with real marine datasets and learn how to:

Assess genome assemblies and reference resources

Process sequencing data (QC, mapping, variant calling, filtering)

Perform population genomics analyses (genetic diversity, structure, demography)

Integrate genomic and environmental data (seascape genomics)

Run and interpret environment?genomics association analyses
 
More information and registration details here: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/marine-genomics/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/marine-genomics/ )
 
Best regards,
Carlo


--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org

mobile: +49 17645230846

[ Bluesky ]( https://bsky.app/profile/physaliacourses.bsky.social ) [ Linkedin ]( https://www.linkedin.com/in/physalia-courses-a64418127/ )

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Hybrid course: GLMMs and GAMMs with R – Townsville
messages.subject_clean: Hybrid course: GLMMs and GAMMs with R – Townsville
messages.body_plain:
Dear colleagues,

We?re happy to announce an upcoming hybrid course:

Introduction to GLMMs and GAMMs with R. - With applications to spatial, 
temporal, and spatial-temporal data-
2?6 March 2026, James Cook University, Townsville, Australia (or join 
online via Zoom)

This is a hands-on, applied course designed for people who want to use 
GLMMs and GAMMs in practice, rather than get lost in theory or heavy 
maths. The aim is to help you understand what these models are doing, 
how to fit them in R, and how to interpret and visualise the results.

We start with a short refresher on multiple linear regression and 
generalised linear models, then move on to generalised additive models 
(GAMs) ? a flexible and intuitive way to deal with non-linear 
relationships. From there, we introduce mixed-effects models for grouped 
or repeated-measures data, and finally bring everything together in 
generalised additive mixed models (GAMMs).

In the final part of the course, we apply these models to spatial, 
temporal, and spatial-temporal data, using realistic examples with 
different types of response variables, including continuous and count data.

Across five modules, we cover:

* How GAMs work, including model selection and smoother interactions
* Linear mixed-effects models and Gaussian additive mixed models
* Hierarchical GAMs (the GAM equivalent of random-slope models)
* Poisson and negative binomial GLMMs and GAMMs
* Applications to spatial and spatial-temporal data
* Guidance on analysing binary, proportional, and continuous data (time 
allowing)

All analyses are done in R, mainly using the mgcv and glmmTMB packages.

Each module includes practical exercises with real data and fully 
annotated R scripts. Before the course starts, you?ll also get access to 
preparatory materials (short videos and exercises) covering linear 
regression, Poisson GLMs, DHARMa model validation, and variograms. PDF 
copies of all theory slides are provided as well.

As part of the course, we also include a free 1-hour one-to-one video 
meeting, where you can discuss your own data or analysis questions with 
one or both instructors.

Venue: James Cook University, Townsville Campus, Australia (hybrid format)
Dates & times: 2?6 March 2026, 09.00?16.00 (local time)
Price: ?500
Instructors: Dr. Alain Zuur and Dr. Elena Ieno

Flyer and registration: https://www.highstat.com/


Please feel free to share this with colleagues who might find it useful.

Kind regards,
Alain Zuur
Highland Statistics Ltd.

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Master Species Distribution Models (SDMs) and Ecological Niche Models (ENMs) with these 3 courses!
messages.subject_clean: Master Species Distribution Models (SDMs) and Ecological Niche Models (ENMs) with these 3 courses!
messages.body_plain:
Want to master Species Distribution Models (SDMs) and Ecological Niche
Models (ENMs)?

PR Stats has you covered.

Species Distribution Modelling and Ecological Niche Modelling are now
central tools in ecology, conservation biology, biogeography, and
environmental decision-making. From predicting species ranges under climate
change to informing conservation planning and invasion biology, robust
modelling is essential. PR Stats offers a comprehensive learning pathway
designed to take you from foundational principles to advanced statistical
approaches.


Standard Modelling Procedure for Species Distribution and Ecological Niche
Modelling

This course lays the groundwork for sound modelling practice. You?ll learn
the complete SDM/ENM workflow, including data preparation, variable
selection, model calibration, validation, and interpretation. Emphasis is
placed on avoiding common pitfalls and following reproducible, defensible
modelling standards that align with current best practices.

https://prstats.org/course/standard-modelling-procedure-for-species-distribution-and-ecological-niche-modelling-sdms01/


Species Distribution Modelling (SDMs) and Ecological Niche Modelling (ENMs)

Building on the foundations, this course explores a wider range of
modelling techniques and applications. You?ll gain hands-on experience with
different algorithms, understand how to interpret outputs critically, and
learn how to match modelling choices to ecological questions. Real-world
examples illustrate how SDMs and ENMs are used in contemporary research and
applied ecology.

https://prstats.org/course/species-distribution-modelling-sdms-and-ecological-niche-modelling-enms-sdmr07/


Species Distribution Modelling with Bayesian Statistics

For those ready to go further, this advanced course introduces Bayesian
approaches to species distribution modelling. Learn how to explicitly model
uncertainty, incorporate prior knowledge, and produce more transparent and
statistically rigorous inferences. Bayesian SDMs are especially powerful
when working with sparse data, complex ecological processes, or when
decision-making requires clear uncertainty estimates.

https://prstats.org/course/species-distribution-modelling-with-bayesian-statistics-sdmb08/


Together, these three courses form a structured learning journey?from core
concepts and workflows to advanced statistical modelling?designed for
students, researchers, conservation practitioners, and environmental
professionals. Whether you are new to SDMs or looking to strengthen and
modernize your modelling toolkit, PR Stats provides practical,
research-informed training you can apply immediately to your own ecological
data.

Discounts for booking 2 or more courses.

Learn better. Model smarter. Make stronger ecological inferences with PR
Stats.
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions.


Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Exploratory Analysis of Complex Datasets with ChatGPT 2.0 – last chance
messages.subject_clean: Exploratory Analysis of Complex Datasets with ChatGPT 2.0 – last chance
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance to join our 1-day seminar Exploratory Analysis of Complex Datasets with ChatGPT 2.0 livestreaming February 12 with Nikolay Oskolkov (Group Leader (PI) at LIOS). This seminar offers advanced techniques for exploratory analysis of complex datasets, including dimensionality reduction, clustering, and data integration. Ecologists can apply these methods to uncover hidden patterns in high-dimensional environmental variables, identify distinct ecological communities, or integrate diverse datasets from vegetation, soils, and hydrology. Furthermore, leveraging tools like R, Python, and ChatGPT will empower researchers to interpret intricate ecological data and refine their analytical workflows.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/exploratory-analysis-of-complex-datasets-2) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
